shortDescription: Keyhole surgery to rebuild a torn knee ligament and restore stability for active movement.

sections.overview:
ACL reconstruction rebuilds a torn anterior cruciate ligament in the knee using a graft, restoring the stability needed for pivoting, turning, and active movement. Patients from Africa, the Middle East, and Asia often choose Korea because accredited Korean hospitals combine arthroscopic (keyhole) techniques, modern graft options, and structured return-to-sport rehabilitation within one coordinated pathway.

The anterior cruciate ligament helps keep the knee stable during twisting and changes of direction. When it tears, often during sport or a sudden pivot, the knee can feel like it gives way, which limits activity and can lead to further joint damage over time. Reconstruction is usually recommended for active patients, or those with ongoing instability, after the initial swelling has settled and movement has returned.

The surgeon replaces the torn ligament with a graft, most commonly taken from your own body (an autograft). Two widely used choices are the hamstring tendon graft and the patellar-tendon (bone-tendon-bone) graft, each with its own characteristics for fixation and recovery, selected to suit your anatomy and activity goals. The procedure is performed arthroscopically through small incisions, which supports a smoother early recovery, and any related cartilage or meniscus damage can be addressed at the same time.

KmedTour coordinates the full journey, including pre-arrival imaging review, interpreter support, hospital admission, and a written rehabilitation plan you can hand to a physiotherapist at home. The aim is a stable knee and a clear, realistic, months-long rehabilitation path, planned around your travel dates and your home-country follow-up so that recovery continues safely after you return.
sections.candidacy:
You may be a candidate for ACL reconstruction if you have a confirmed anterior cruciate ligament tear with knee instability, a sense of the knee giving way, or difficulty returning to sport, work, or active daily life. Imaging, usually an MRI, confirms the tear and shows any related meniscus or cartilage damage. Candidates are typically active adults, or people whose instability affects everyday movement.

Surgery is usually planned once the initial swelling has settled and the knee has regained good movement and muscle control, which improves the outcome of reconstruction and rehabilitation. The surgeon discusses graft choice, commonly a hamstring or patellar-tendon autograft, in relation to your anatomy and activity goals.

Before approving surgery, the team reviews your general health, including fitness for anaesthesia, diabetes control, and any blood-clotting or bleeding history. Active infection, a very stiff or swollen knee, or unstable medical conditions may delay surgery until they are managed. Because the rehabilitation that follows is long and central to success, good candidates are committed to a structured physiotherapy program over several months. A pre-travel video consultation and your recent scans let the surgeon confirm suitability and plan the graft before you fly.
sections.procedure_steps:
ACL reconstruction is performed arthroscopically, meaning the surgeon works through small incisions using a camera and fine instruments rather than opening the knee fully. You receive either a general or a spinal anaesthetic, often with a nerve block for pain relief afterward.

The surgeon first inspects the joint, confirms the ligament tear, and treats any related meniscus or cartilage damage. The torn ligament tissue is cleared to prepare the site.

A graft is then prepared. With a hamstring autograft, one or two hamstring tendons are taken from the same leg and folded into a strong cord. With a patellar-tendon, or bone-tendon-bone, autograft, the surgeon takes the middle portion of the patellar tendon along with small bone blocks at each end, which can give secure bone-to-bone healing in the tunnels. The choice depends on your anatomy and activity goals.

The surgeon drills precise tunnels in the thighbone and shinbone to match the natural attachment points of the original ligament, then passes the graft through these tunnels and fixes it under appropriate tension using devices such as screws or buttons. The new ligament is tested through a range of motion to confirm the knee is stable and tracks correctly. The small incisions are closed and dressed, and clot-prevention measures begin. The procedure commonly takes one to two hours, after which you are monitored in recovery before returning to the ward.
sections.recovery_timeline:
Recovery is structured around your seven-day stay and the longer rehabilitation that follows at home. Early physiotherapy usually begins within the first day, focusing on regaining straightening, gentle bending, controlled weight-bearing with crutches as advised, and activating the thigh muscles. A brace may be used to protect the graft in the early phase.

During the week, the team manages swelling and pain, checks the wounds, and progresses your movement and walking under guidance. Before you travel, the surgeon confirms the wounds are healing and that you can move safely on crutches.

Most patients are advised to confirm with the surgeon before flying, because longer flights carry a clot risk after surgery. You will receive guidance on compression, hydration, moving your ankles during the flight, keeping the leg moving, and any prescribed medication.

Before departure, KmedTour provides a written, staged rehabilitation plan, your operative summary, and graft details so a physiotherapist at home can continue your program. ACL rehabilitation is gradual and typically spans several months, progressing from regaining motion and strength to balance, agility, and finally sport-specific drills. A return to pivoting sports is usually considered only after many months, once strength and control targets are met and the surgeon or your home physiotherapist agrees. Arrange a local follow-up review, and report increasing pain, redness, fever, or calf swelling promptly.
sections.cost_breakdown:
The estimated cost for ACL reconstruction through KmedTour ranges from $5,000 to $12,000. Where you fall in this range depends on the graft chosen, the fixation devices used, whether additional meniscus or cartilage repair is needed, and the length of your hospital stay.

The quoted price generally covers the surgeon and anaesthesia fees, the arthroscopic procedure, operating-room use, implants such as fixation devices, your inpatient stay, standard medications, and inpatient physiotherapy during admission. Pre-operative tests and assessments are usually included once you arrive.

Items normally not included are international flights, accommodation before or after your hospital stay, meals outside the hospital, a knee brace if recommended, and the extended months of rehabilitation and follow-up care once you return home. If both knees or additional structures are treated, the cost will be higher.

KmedTour provides a written, itemised quotation after the surgeon reviews your scans, so you can see what is and is not covered before you commit. We also help arrange interpreter support and local accommodation so you can budget for the full trip rather than the surgery alone.
sections.why_korea:
Korea is a well-established destination for orthopaedic and sports surgery, with major hospitals accredited under national and international standards and overseen within a healthcare system regulated by the Ministry of Health and Welfare. KOIHA accreditation and KHIDI's support for international patients reflect a country-level focus on quality and patient safety for visitors.

Many Korean hospitals offer arthroscopic ligament reconstruction using established graft options and modern fixation, with experienced sports-medicine and rehabilitation services. Physiotherapy is integrated into the surgical pathway from the first day after surgery, and rehabilitation plans are structured to guide the long return-to-activity process.

For international patients, Korean hospitals provide coordinated international patient departments, interpreter services, and structured discharge planning. KmedTour adds end-to-end coordination, from pre-arrival imaging review to a written, staged rehabilitation handover for your home physiotherapist, so that care planned in Korea continues smoothly once you return to your home country.

faqs.q: How will I continue physiotherapy after I return home?
faqs.a: ACL recovery depends heavily on rehabilitation, so before you leave Korea KmedTour gives you a written, staged plan, your operative summary, and graft details to hand to a local physiotherapist. Your program continues at home under their supervision over several months, progressing from regaining motion and strength to balance and agility. Arrange a follow-up review with a local clinic so progress is monitored and milestones are checked in person.

faqs.q: Is it safe to fly home after ACL surgery?
faqs.a: Confirm with your surgeon before flying, as longer flights carry a clot risk after surgery. Once cleared, you will receive guidance on compression, staying hydrated, moving your ankles regularly, keeping the leg moving, and taking aisle breaks where possible. You will also be advised how to manage crutches and any brace during travel and how to keep the knee comfortable on a long journey.

faqs.q: What is done to prevent blood clots and DVT?
faqs.a: Clot prevention begins after surgery and continues through your stay and travel. Measures may include early movement, ankle exercises, compression, staying hydrated, and any prescribed medication. The team will advise how long to continue precautions after you return home. Report calf pain, swelling, redness, or breathlessness promptly, as these can signal a clot that needs prompt assessment, especially around the time of a long flight.

faqs.q: Should I choose a hamstring or patellar-tendon graft?
faqs.a: Both are autografts taken from your own leg and are widely used. A hamstring graft uses folded hamstring tendons, while a patellar-tendon (bone-tendon-bone) graft includes small bone blocks that can give secure healing in the tunnels. Each has its own characteristics for fixation and recovery. The surgeon recommends the option best suited to your anatomy and activity goals and discusses it with you before surgery.

faqs.q: When can I return to sport after ACL reconstruction?
faqs.a: Return to pivoting sports is gradual and usually considered only after many months, not weeks. Rehabilitation progresses through stages, regaining motion and strength, then balance and agility, then sport-specific drills, before any return to competition. The timing depends on meeting strength and control targets and on agreement from the surgeon or your home physiotherapist. Returning too early raises the risk of re-injury, so milestones matter more than dates.
